Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Terrence Heaslett, Esq., of Wood Smith Henning & Berman LLP, (619) 849-4900, for Defendants.
No other appearances.

Not argued/contested. At the outset, the court recognizes its inadvertent issuance of an incorrect tentative ruling and its notice of a modified tentative ruling to the parties via email as soon as the court discovered its inadvertence. The court then states that it is inclined to adopt the modified tentative ruling. No objection to the preceding is presented. Accordingly, the court adopts the following tentative ruling: Defendant's demurrer to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT is sustained with leave to amend.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the modified tentative ruling and must deliver it to Dept. 301.

Judge: Christopher C. Hite. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CCH)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-31-2025 Continued To Mar-11-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-20-2025 Continued To Jan-21-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-26-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANTS MCLANE SUNEAST D/B/A MCLANE PACIFIC, ROBERTO RUBALCABA, and CLAIMS SERVICE, INC. DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.
The court adopts its tentative ruling. Demurrer to Plaintiffs Brenda Marzal and Aaron Volponi's first amended complaint/ SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND.

1. The issue under CCP section 430.10(e) is, taking the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed as true, does the challenged cause of action necessarily fail to state a claim for relief. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.)
2. In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.)
3. Section 430.10(f) applies when the pleading is uncertain, including ambiguous and unintelligible. Section 430.10(g) applies when, "[i]n an action founded upon a contract, it cannot be ascertained from the pleading whether the contract is written, is oral, or is implied by conduct."
4. Regardless, the court liberally construes the complaint pursuant to CCP section 452.

First Cause of Action/Breach of Contract
1. To plead a claim for breach of contract, Plaintiff must allege the "(1) the contract, (2) plaintiff's performance or excuse for nonperformance, (3) defendant's breach, and (4) the resulting damages to plaintiff." (Reichart v. Gen. Ins. Co. (1968) 68 Cal.2d 822, 830.)
2. Here, Plaintiffs have failed to allege what contract forms the basis for this claim or how Plaintiffs performed under said contract.
3. Although the FAC mentions breach and damages, it does so in only conclusory terms.

Second cause of action/Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
1. A breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires that (1) the plaintiff and the defendant entered into a contract; (2) the plaintiff did substantially all of the significant things that the contract required him to do; (3) that all conditions required for the defendant's performance had occurred; (4) that the defendant's conduct prevented the plaintiff from receiving the benefits under the contract; (5) by doing so, the defendant did not act fairly and in good faith; and (6) the plaintiff was harmed by the defendant's conduct. (CACI 325.)

2. Here, Plaintiffs do not sufficiently allege any of the elements of a claim for breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
3. As it appears the defects in pleading have a reasonable possibility of being cured by amendment, Plaintiffs are granted leave to amend.

Third cause of action/Negligence
1. "The elements of a cause of action for negligence are (1) a legal duty to use reasonable care, (2) breach of that duty, and (3) proximate cause between the breach and (4) the plaintiff's injury." (Mendoza v. City of Los Angeles (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 1333, 1339).
2. Here, Plaintiffs do not allege any element of negligence other than damages, which is only alleged in conclusory terms. However, plaintiffs will be given the opportunity to remedy the defects.
3. In addition, the Court finds that Plaintiffs' pleading is uncertain as it does not distinguish which causes of actions apply to which defendant(s).
4. Also, Plaintiffs' claims are based upon an alleged contract, but it cannot be ascertained from the amended pleading whether the alleged contract is written, is oral, or is implied by conduct.

Motion to strike/Denied.
1. Defendants must file a separate motion to strike under CCP section 435.
2. Motion denied without prejudice to renew under a properly filed separate motion.

Proposed order is submitted.

Judge: Barbara Zuniga. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/BZ)
